speaker: interviewee
text: The Jugendheim was-- I mean, the whole endeavor of Fredy Hirsch to build this out of nothing in this really terrible place, and to protect the children, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it's really the highest of achievements. Although, the achievement is gone, because the children were all killed except the hundred Birkenau boys, because all of us remember  this Jugendheim. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And the Jugendheim, they-- they had wonderful activities. And of course, everything that was done is only remembered by the surviving madrichim, the hundred boys, and that's all, because the children are all gone. Ariel Edelstein is all gone-- is gone. Anyway, I was-- I was stoker. Now we had-- we could write-- write letters to the Red Cross. We were told what to write, so on. We had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you what to write?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were given instructions through the blockalteste that each family could write to an address. And we could write abroad, anywhere. So we wrote to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that postcard arrived-- and we asked for parcel. And they said, you can ask for food parcels, and the Red Cross will send food parcels. And the food parcels arrived to this family camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, that's why people are saying-- people are saying that the family camp was going to be inspected by the Red Cross, because it was a show camp. We also had better rations than the rest of Birkenau. I will tell you what the rations were. A quarter of a loaf of bread. And to start with, we had a dollop of marmalade every third day. And we had a dollop of cheese once a week. And then we had the soup, and we had the tea. I wonder how many of the grownups got the message when the jam and the margarine and the cheese-- it was sort of ersatz cheese, but nevertheless it was fat, and it was nourishing-- when that was done away with and you only had the bread without anything, whether people drew the right conclusion from that. You know, because the camp was liquidated. But I certainly didn't draw that conclusion at the time. I remember that I did not draw the conclusion. I only-- I was too busy trying to steal food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality were the other prisoners in that--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all Czech. That was the-- that was all Theresienstadt stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans were in charge. And there were kapos. And there was the lageralteste, who was a political German prisoner who was a prisoner from a prison who, presumably, volunteered to be in charge of a camp of 10,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in this Jugendheim. And these Red Cross parcels arrived. By the time that the Red Cross parcels arrived, lots of people who had asked for parcels [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had already died of natural causes, because within six months-- I now read this. I didn't know then-- but I now read that a third of us died within six months, of natural causes. Just because of the privation of cold and mud, and standing in the mud, you know, to these appels. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember the first appel, that morning that I was describing to you. Sure fantastic scene. I was standing next to a man, and he was clutching in his hand a syringe and insulin. So I asked him, you know, what's this for? So he said, this is my last insulin. Tomorrow I'll die.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he had a half open vial. And he kept this open vial overnight to give himself another injection in the morning. And that was the last insulin. And he told me, tomorrow I'll die. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] To get back to the Jugendheim, these Red Cross parcels that were now not deliverable because the people were dead, actually stayed in the camp. The Germans were quite correct about it. They stayed in the camp, and they were brought to the Jugendheim to give the children. And sort of sugar and sweets and so on were distributed amongst the children. And then there were tins, you know. All kind-- people send all kinds of tins-- sardines, anything, beans, soups.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Fredy brought these-- a box of tins and a bag of flour. And he said, you know how to make a roux? You know, he's-- here's some margarine. He gave me salt. He says, you know how to salt a soup? So I said, yeah. I know everything. You know, it's-- I'll make soup. So he says, throw everything together, one pot. He gave me a big pot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this oven, by the way, was a big thing, you know. You could build the fire on the side and then put a pot in. So we did another pot where I was-- was heating the oven. And Fredy said, look, it's your job to make the soup. And it's your job to make the roux. And you-- make the soup really thick so that the children have something to eat. And then put in salt. Make sure you don't over salt it, because it's most important you don't over salt. And when the soup is done, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we'll-- we'll issue the soup to the-- to the children. And it's your job to see that the soup tastes well, so you can taste it. So I was tasting soup. And, you know, when you're hungry, you taste a bit of soup, and it was very good. It was really like this potato soup that my mom made, because you make a roux, you know, and it becomes all brown and thick. There were no potatoes in it, but there were beans in it. And the soup was absolutely terrific. I mean, I made a really good soup. And I tasted it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was sort of-- I was quite busy every day making soup and supervising Ariel, who couldn't-- he couldn't do anything right, poor chap. But anyway, we made soup. Can I ask--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was satisfactory, except one day-- well, one day the blockalteste from opposite, from KB-- that's Krankenbau. Krankenbau means the hospital-- sees me sort of running around making soup. So he says, I want you to be laufer for me. You know, my runner in the Krankenbau. So I said to him, look, I've got work here. I'm making soup. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So two or three days later, I'm hauled up in front of a sort of judge and jury. Fredy is there. And it's all a bit of a joke. And Fredy says, I'm told you steal soup. So I say, I steal soup? I am tasting soup. [LAUGHS] So he says, yes, I told you to taste soup. So he says, I'll tell you what, Micha-- he spoke only German, you see-- I tell you what, we'll have to get somebody else to have a chance to taste soup. You've lost your job, because you tasted too much soup. [LAUGHS] And I went straight out of his room to the block opposite, to this blockalteste of the Krankenbau, and I said, is the position still vacant? [LAUGHS] And he says, yes, I haven't found a runner. So he said, fine. You can sleep here, and it's-- I'm going to arrange it. Before you carry on, can I just ask you where was Milos at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Milos was in Block 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were reunited?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whilst I was in the Jugendheim, I was with him in Block 17. And Milos was on a detail called rollwagen. Rollwagen was a terrible detail. Have you ever heard what a rollwagen is?

speaker: interviewer
text: I haven't.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know the haywick-type of wagon where you have two sides at 45 degrees with sort of laths from the-- and you can put hay in it? And it's like a-- it's like a ladder, like a big ladder. And of course, wheels. And what they used to have in this mud, they had 16 people pull this wick. And the wick was used for all kinds of transport. Transport of the soup to the various blocks inside the camp, and transport of the dead to the crematoria. That was the worst one, by the way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Perhaps I'll tell you about that a bit later. It's a horrendous thing. This rollwagen that went-- I-- I saw one when I was laufer in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't see-- and luckily Milos wasn't on that rollwagen. But Milos was on some sort of rollwagen taking goods. The goods that were being confiscated were being taken from one place to Canada. So here and there, he got some food from somebody who would hand him something. But Milos was getting terribly thin. He suddenly grew a head taller. You know, he started growing. And he was what we call a Muselmann. He was getting so thin and emaciated that he was too weak. The point about the rollwagen was that you started with 16. If somebody fell or died or was beaten to death whilst pulling, then there were 15. And it's like the story of 10 Little Indians. You know, one rolls over, and there are nine little red Indians and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the rollwagen got more and more difficult towards the evening when they invariably came without one or two people who expired because of the-- particularly through the winter. It was-- the winter of '43, '44 was a very severe winter. I remember because this frost was outside. And we were in this heat-- the only block which was heated was this little-- this this block number 29, which was the Jugendheim. So I got a job opposite, because I was told that I was stealing soup. I was tasting soup. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I started in-- I started in business. [LAUGHS] I-- it-- it's such a fantastic thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was dysentery in the camp. So when you had dysentery, the one thing that you had to do is not to go to the hospital. Because in the hospital, your dysentery would get worse. The only advantage in the hospital was that your ration was white bread instead of black bread. And with dysentery, you couldn't eat the black bread anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got a ration of white bread, because I was a runner in the hospital. And I started running around the camp selling my white bread for three rations of black bread. And I was-- would buy these three rations from people who had dysentery, who had dysentery for three days and they hadn't eaten nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: They could only eat white bread, because if they would eat this awful black bread-- the black bread was made with chestnuts. So it was practically indigestible. But if you were hungry enough, you-- you ate the black bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were nights when I got a whole loaf of bread out of this profit, because I'd go back to the Krankenbau barrack, where I had access because I was the runner there, and I would sell black bread to dysentery patients who were already getting better. But I would give them two portions of black bread for one portion of white bread. And then I'd go back and demand three portions of black bread. So sometimes-- I did that about three times every evening, and I came away with a whole loaf of bread. And I'd carry this to Milos. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- oh, there was another thing. I was offered to sleep in the Krankenbau, and I was given a porridge in the Krankenbau, which was a white soup. It was actually quite palatable, the porridge. Much better than the soup in the camp. The soup in the camp I could never eat, because it was made with rotten potatoes. And it smelled. And it was made in these big beer barrels. It was transported in beer barrels, and the beer barrels got completely black on the inside from the soup rotting from day to day. And that's why people caught dysentery, you know. It was a sort of rolling effect. And the fact that the ration went down, you know, because at first we got 1,500 calories. And then, with the bread, we got 1,000 calories. And with 1,000 calories, you'll slowly lose weight. You know, go and lose weight with Weight Watchers. [LAUGHS] I'm-- I'm an expert with weight watching. At the 1,000 calories, you lose weight. So Milos was losing weight. Leo was losing weight. Leo would never take a piece of bread from me, because he was the grownup. And I was now a runner in the hospital. I said, come and be doctor in the hospital. You know, I'm going-- I'll talk to the man in charge, you know, of the doctors there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get transferred to the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, inside the camp, it was more or less free. Again, it was inside administration, so it could be arranged. And Leo refused. He wasn't going to-- you know, he had cooperated in Theresienstadt, but he was not going to cooperate in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your duties as a runner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, well, the first day I got-- I got the band. You know, I was a runner. And I got a little-- a bit of a nicer jacket. You must look a bit nicer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it a bit more? What was the band like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it said 'Laufer, KB.' 'Laufer' means runner. 'KB,' KB is Krankenbau.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the afternoon, Mengele comes in his staff car. And he drove right into the camp. He was-- quite a fearless thing to do, in fact, you know, because we could have done him in. He came unarmed. How did you recognize him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't. I was just told who he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the name mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. He was just an officer. And it was the first afternoon that I was there. And there was an operation. A man came in with a crushed hand. All this mud that was outside, they were building a road just by putting a stone foundation so that you could drive through the camp with cars or-- so they were making these stone foundations. And somebody came with a crushed hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, could you just tell me who-- who that person was? Was it a German officer or a prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a prisoner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prisoner.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish-- Jewish man. And the Krankenbau-- Krankenbau, it's-- it's laughable that there was a hospital, because people just came there to die. It was an assembly point to die. Particularly with the dysentery in the block. But in the front of the block, on the chimney, they put a white sheet and they started an operation. So they washed his hand. The man was screaming blue murder. And you couldn't help but watch, you see. It's-- I'd never seen a hospital operation. And Mengele walked in and he said, weitermachen. Weithermachen, continue. And he stood somewhere behind. And I was fascinated by this. And suddenly I feel queasy, you know, because the-- the flesh is falling away, and they're picking this bit of finger and that bit of finger. And I feel a bit queasy, and I collapse. And I come to, something is holding me by the scuffle of the neck. And a white glove is waving a bottle of ammonia in front of my face. And I sort of squirm away, you know, to try and get away from this. And this voice says, [GERMAN] You know, you just watch. You'll get used to it. And it was Mengele who was holding me. In order to deal with prisoners, he always wore these white gloves, you see. After he handled me, he took the white gloves off, put them on the chimney. And he does his inspection. You know, they go and see the dysentery. Another man comes in, and he's got hernia. Now, they finished this operation. And I still feel sort of, you know, sleepy and-- you don't feel well after you've collapsed. In Yiddish you say it's tsemisht, which is a lovely word, you know. [LAUGHS] 'Tsemisht' means mixed up. You're all mixed up. And They bring this man in, and he's got his sack between his legs right down to his knees like this. Hernia. And this Dr. Heller-- as it happens, also Dr-- a Heller. It's a common Jewish name. A red-haired man. And he was some sort of professor from the University of Brno, which was Leo's university. And Leo knew him, and wouldn't go and work with him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What Leo's reasons were, I don't know. And he started stuffing this hernia in, and within about three or four minutes, the man was lying there normal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they bring out boxes of belts, and they have thousands of belts, you know, hernia belts. And they try this one and try that one.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've probably never seen a hernia belt. Have you seen a hernia belt? I have.

speaker: interviewee
text: You now, they are made with this special steel which is bent and sprung to fit your body. So all the belts that they were trying on this man were loose. And you can't bend it, because there's no heat, you know, to-- to temper steel. There's no way of bending it. I mean, they tried to bend it, but it's spring steel. It's-- it's not possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mengele says to the doctor, send him tomorrow. And he writes on a piece of paper a prescription that I should bring small hernia belts. That you should bring?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You know, you asked me what the tasks were. So I went to a place called Canada. Why Canada? Because in Canada, everything was available, including hernia belts. You know, because everything was taken out these thousands and thousands of people that were arriving every week. And everything was sorted-- glasses, watches, jewelry, clothes, shoes, hernia belts. There was-- there was a big store of hernia belts. You'd be surprised how many people had a hernia and bring hernia belts into Auschwitz. Anyway, he goes out to the staff car. And I admire him how he jumps from one stone to the next. He hardly gets his boots wet from the mud. And he's at the car and he bellows, laufer. So when you hear laufer, you have to run and report. You know, laufer [INAUDIBLE]. I was told what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I go and report. And he bellows something at me. And I speak perfect German. I understand German perfectly. But I was, like I told you, tsemisht after I had-- and I had enough presence of mind to run and salute and sort of say, I'm reporting for duty. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Actually, I was trying to do what he did, you know, jump from one stone to the next to avoid the mud. And I went right in the mud, but I continued running, you know, because he wanted me. And so that was it. You had to be-- instantaneous obedience was the sort of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] way to behave. And you just had to copy what other prisoners did, you see? And he bellows some command. And I have no idea what he says. Now, it's fatal to say, what? You know, I beg your pardon? You know? You lose your job if you-- so I turn around and I say, ja wohl. And I run back to the-- to the block.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm thinking, now I'm for it, because I don't know what the hell he said. And this doctor says, in Czech, he says to me, he wants his gloves. They're on the chimney. [LAUGHS] So I grab the gloves and bring the gloves. And that's how I started being a runner. And Mengele had a task for me every time he came. He came once a week to inspect the hospital. And he had one or other type of errand for me, like bringing belts for this man. This man was told, hold yourself, you know. Until we get a belt for you, hold yourself. And then when I brought the other belts, they were all too big for him as well-- although I had selected the smallest belts-- because he was so emaciated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What other work did he give you to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he once sent-- he and Heller-- he treated Dr. Heller with-- Mengele treated Dr. Heller with great respect. He called him Dr. Heller. Usually you address the prisoner just by his number, but he called him Dr. Heller, because he was a senior surgeon this man. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they had a bet that-- I forget the medicine. It might have been aspirin. I don't know what it was. But Dr. Heller wanted a medicine, and he asked Mengele, you know, to write a prescription and that medicine should be bought. And Mengele said, look, I'll give you a prescription, and you can keep the medicine on one condition. I mean, it'll be issued, because its a prescription, but you can keep the medicine on one condition. If the guard likes him and tells me that she likes him-- it's a nurse, an SS nurse-- if she likes him-- and they made it all into a joke, you know, that if she likes him and she says he's a nice boy, you can keep the medicine. And if she hates him, because he's a Jewish frechling-- 'frechling' means cheeky, cheeky, good-for-nothing-- then you'll give the medicine back to me. So it was a sort of game, you know, that he was going to issue the medicine, but Dr. Heller was not allowed to use it until he said, you can use it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to see this nurse in the gypsy camp. And this was my second venture outside our abschnitt. It was a great thing to be outside on the road and have the orientation how Auschwitz was made up, and where the crematoria were and where the sauna was, you know, and where the kommandatura was and so on. And how-- how-- how you walked out of the gate, you know. What the whole procedure was.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot you knew that other people were just incarcerated. So it was a terrific advantage that you could move about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we stop here a moment.
